coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic carbon dots modified porous thermoplastic polyurethane for effective and durable oil/water separation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yuting
Pan, Lan
Jiang, Kejin
Wang, Qianhua
Zhu, Zhijie
Tian, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Porous thermoplastic polyurethane (PTPU) materials have shown potential for oil/water separation due to their high porosity, good elasticity and repeatability. It is urgent to find an appreciated method that modifies the surface properties of PTPU so as to improve the separation efficiency by effectively enhancing the interaction between the surface and organic pollutant. In this paper, hydrophobic carbon dots (CDs) were applied to modify PTPU (PTPU/CDs) to improve the oil/water separation performance. As a result of optimization of the preparation of PTPU and the addition of CDs, the hydrophobicity (from 120° to 138.1°), compressive strength (from 186 to 233 kPa) and saturated absorption capacity (from 2.4–23 to 4–39 g −1) were improved significantly compared with pure PTPU. In addition, in this process, the PTPU/CDs composites were endowed with bright yellow fluorescence due to the CDs, making it possible to track the oil absorption progress under ultraviolet irradiation. Furthermore, the PTPU/CDs composites demonstrated exceptional durability and environmental adaptability in the process of treating oily-water. Additionally, the absorption kinetics demonstrated that the PTPU/CDs material showed an approximately 1.5-fold increase in absorption rate compared with PTPU. When used as filter membrane, the PTPU/CDs composites could effectively separate surfactant-stabilized toluene-in-water emulsion with up to 99% efficiency, demonstrating an effective way for treatment of various oily wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oil/water separation
Porous materials
Thermoplastic polyurethane
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots
Saturation absorption capacity and absorption kinetic test
Preparation process of PTPU and PTPU/CDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL spectra of the prepared CDs. Insert: optical images of CDs under visible (left) and ultraviolet light (right). (b) TEM image of the prepared CDs. (c) PL spectra of the pure PTPU and the PTPU/CDs. Insert: optical images of pure PTPU and the PTPU/CDs-2 under ultraviolet light. (d-f) SEM images of the PTPU/CDs-1, PTPU/CDs-2, PTPU/CDs-3.
Water contact angels of the PTPU, PTPU/CDs-1, PTPU/CDs-2 and PTPU/CDs-3.
Stress–strain curves of (a) PTPU and (b) PTPU/CDs-2.

(a) Statistics of the saturation absorption capability (Qs) of PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2 on various type of solvents and oil. The cyclic saturation absorption of peanut oil carried out by manual extrusion (b) and the recyclability test results (c). (d) Separation processes of soybean oil floating on water by repeated squeezing and absorption. (e) The separation efficiency of PTPU/CDs-2 for soybean oil floating on water during 30 cycles. (f) The tetrachloromethane at bottom of water was removed by PTPU/CDs-2. (g) The peanut oil (stained by methyl red) was separated from water under intense stirring. (h) Photo of droplets of different pH on the surface of PTPU/CDs-2.
The oil absorption process of PTPU/CDs-2 under (a) visible and (b) ultraviolet light.

(a, b) Photographs of the surfactant stabilized toluene-in-water emulsion before and after separation. (c) Absorbance spectra of the feed emulsion with different dilution ratio. (d) Absorbance intensities at 261 nm as a function of toluene concentrations.

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic carbon dots modified porous thermoplastic polyurethane for effective and durable oil/water separation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Advanced Textile Engineering Technology Center, Jiangsu College of Engineering and Technology, Nantong, Jiangsu 226007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Advanced Textile Engineering Technology Center
Jiangsu College of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bJiangsu Advanced Textile Engineering Technology Center, Jiangsu College of Engineering and Technology, Nantong, Jiangsu 226007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porous thermoplastic polyurethane (PTPU) materials have shown potential for oil/water separation due to their high porosity, good elasticity and repeatability. It is urgent to find an appreciated method that modifies the surface properties of PTPU so as to improve the separation efficiency by effectively enhancing the interaction between the surface and organic pollutant. In this paper, hydrophobic carbon dots (CDs) were applied to modify PTPU (PTPU/CDs) to improve the oil/water separation performance. As a result of optimization of the preparation of PTPU and the addition of CDs, the hydrophobicity (from 120° to 138.1°), compressive strength (from 186 to 233 kPa) and saturated absorption capacity (from 2.4–23 to 4–39 g −1) were improved significantly compared with pure PTPU. In addition, in this process, the PTPU/CDs composites were endowed with bright yellow fluorescence due to the CDs, making it possible to track the oil absorption progress under ultraviolet irradiation. Furthermore, the PTPU/CDs composites demonstrated exceptional durability and environmental adaptability in the process of treating oily-water. Additionally, the absorption kinetics demonstrated that the PTPU/CDs material showed an approximately 1.5-fold increase in absorption rate compared with PTPU. When used as filter membrane, the PTPU/CDs composites could effectively separate surfactant-stabilized toluene-in-water emulsion with up to 99% efficiency, demonstrating an effective way for treatment of various oily wastewater.

Carbon dots modified porous thermoplastic polyurethane sponges with high organic solvent saturated absorption capacity, excellent cycle performance, good environmental adaptability and effective oil/water separation are presented.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oily wastewater poses a serious threat to both the natural environment and human health. Consequently, the extraction and collection of organic pollutants from such wastewater have become a crucial issue in the field of environmental protection. Several conventional methods have been applied for oil-water separation, such as gravity separation [1], dissolved air flotation [2], flocculation technology [3], and electrochemical technology [4]. However, these methods still suffer from low separation efficiency, high cost, or the inability to treat emulsion-like oil-water mixtures [5,6]. Hydrophobic materials with unique wettability have been proven to be effective for oil-water separation and offer significant benefits in terms of both cost and efficiency [7], especially for the separation of oil-water emulsions [8]. Porous materials with three-dimensional structures are ideal materials for oil absorption due to their extensive storage space for pollutants [9]. Porous thermoplastic polyurethane (PTPU) sponge, consisting of diisocyanate (hard segments) and polyol (soft segments), has become a promising candidate for oil-water separation material [10,11] due to its advantages, including easy large-scale preparation, high strength, low density, high porosity, good elasticity, and repeatability [12]. Surface modification techniques have been utilized to enhance the oil-water separation efficiency by improving the adhesion force between PTPU and organic pollutant through reducing the surface energy or enhancing the surface roughness [13]. Several organic substances, such as silane coupling agent [14], and inorganic materials, like SiO2 [15], Fe3O4 [16,17], ZnO [18], and TiO2 [19,20], have been used for this purpose. Sharjeel et al. synthesized hydrophobic SiO2 nanoparticles using the sol-gel method with sodium silicate and trimethylchlorosilane as reactants. TPU deposited with such hydrophobic SiO2 nanoparticles demonstrated high hydrophobicity and effective oil-water separation [21]. Compared to the aforementioned modifiers, carbon-based nanomaterials offer a wider range of raw material sources and lower costs. Up to now, graphene oxide (GO) [22,23], graphitic carbon nitride [24,25] and amorphous carbon nanosphere [26], have been used to construct superhydrophobic surfaces of porous materials for oil separation due to their unique chemical and physical properties. Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based material with size smaller than 10 nm, possess significant fluorescence performance, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, and good biocompatibility [27–29], showing significant prospect in modifying porous materials. Yin et al. used alkylated CDs to modify the surface of TPU material by the dipping method, yielding TPU oil-absorbing sponges with excellent oily water treatment and good durability [30]. Su et al. achieved rapid and enhanced separation of viscous oil spills by loading CDs on the surface of commercial porous sponge, in which the solar harvesting ability and superhydrophobicity of CDs could reduce the viscosity and surface tension of oil by increasing the temperature under irradiation [31]. Despite significant progress in enhancing the hydrophobicity of PTPU through modification with carbon-based materials, considerable attention should be devoted to regulating the pore structure and improving mechanical properties, which play crucial role in expanding the application of the sponges in filtration processes and enhancing their recyclability, respectively. However, the mechanical properties and pore structures of porous materials are predominantly influenced by the choice of raw sponge, (in most cases, commercial sponges). Therefore, prior to the formation of porous materials, carbon-based materials have been incorporated into the polymer precursor solution, which allows for tailoring the pore structure and surface morphology in the resulting porous materials, while also enhancing their strength through interactions with the carbon-based material. In this case, Ye et al. incorporated carboxyl multiwalled carbon nanotubes (cMWNTs) into the TPU matrix to generate TPU/cMWNTs composites [32]. Notably, the modification led to a significant enhancement in the pore structure, including roughness, size distribution and framework, while also greatly improving the mechanical compression and resilience properties of the composites, which thereby benefit the performance in the oil-water separation. However, there is still a pressing need to develop porous materials with not only improved hydrophobicity, but also tailored pore structures and enhanced mechanical properties and particularly novel features like fluorescence tracking capabilities, utilizing cost-effective carbon-based materials, to expand their applications in oil-water emulsion separation and other fields.

In this work, hydrophobic CDs were utilized to modify PTPU to adjust the hydrophobicity, pore structure and mechanical property and the performance of PTPU/CDs composite materials in oil-water separation was investigated. Initially thermally induced phase separation (TIPS) method was applied to prepare PTPU [32]. The influence of polymer concentration, phase separation time, phase separation temperature and the ratio of good to poor solvent on the pore structure and hydrophobicity of PTPU were explored in detail to optimize the experimental conditions. Hydrophobic CDs, synthesized by solvent-free method with long alkyl chain compounds as precursor, were added to the solvent before TPU phase separation. With the help of the interaction of TPU and CDs, an embryonic skeleton was formed, and finally a modified porous material with improved mechanical property and pore structure was obtained. The properties of saturated absorption performance, selective oil-water separation ability and cycle performance of PTPU/CDs composite were systematically investigated. In particular, the ability of the CDs modified TPU porous materials in separation of oil in water emulsion was studied. It is worth mentioning that, with the fluorescence properties of CDs, the obtained PTPU/CDs can generate fluorescence phenomenon under ultraviolet irradiation, possessing special advantages while being used as oil-water separation materials in dark environments.

The 1185A TPU pellets were purchased from BASF Co., Ltd. n-octadecylamine (ODA, >98%), citric acid (AR), 1,4-dioxane (AR), dichloromethane (AR), toluene (>99.5%), acetone (>99.5%), tetrachloromethane (AR), cyclohexane (AR), trichloromethane (>99%), Tween 80 (CP), and methyl red (AR), peanut and soybean oils were obtained from local suppliers and used without any additional treatment.

Thermally induced phase separation method was applied to prepare PTPU materials. The typical operation steps were as follows: 3.2 g of TPU pellets were dissolved in a 40 mL mixture of 1,4-dioxane and deionized water. The mixture was vigorously stirred at 60°C for 2 h by a magnetic stirrer until a uniform solution was obtained. Subsequently, the solution was placed in an ice bath at a temperature of 0°C for 30 min to induce phase separation. The sample was then stored in an environment at the temperature of −20°C for 12 h to complete the phase separation process. Finally, mixed solvents in the frozen samples were removed by freeze-drying at −80°C for 24 h to finally give the PTPU oil-separation materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent free method was used to prepare hydrophobic CDs for the modification of PTPU materials. Typically, 1.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g ODA were grinded and mixed evenly. Then the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200°C for 4 h to complete thermal decomposition. The obtained dark brown product was dispersed in dichloromethane and filtered through a 0.45 μm organic filter to remove unacceptable large particles. The final product was dried and stored in a brown container for further usage.

The PTPU/CD composite materials were prepared using the same procedure for the preparation of PTPU under optimized process conditions except the addition of CDs into the mixture of 1,4-dioxane and deionized water at different concentrations, in the first step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Saturation absorption capacity and absorption kinetic test
ce:section.ce:para:
A specific mass of PTPU was measured and immersed in 10 mL of various solvents. After a certain interval of time, it was taken out and the organic reagents attached to the surface of the sample were delicately wiped off using filter paper. The mass of the PTPU after absorbing solvent was weighed and recorded and returned to the organic solvent again. The above steps were repeated until the weight of the sample no longer changed. The saturation absorption capacity (Qs ) of the sample was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q S
=
M s
−
M 0
M 0
where M0 refers to the initial mass of PTPU material, and Ms refers to the mass of PTPU material at saturation absorption.

The absorption (Qt) over time (t) of PTPU and PTPU/CD composite materials with soybean oil as a model pollutant was measured. The absorption rate depended on the unoccupied space inside the PTPU after absorption. The absorption kinetics of PTPU and PTPU/CD materials on soybean oil was studied using the pseudo-first order reaction kinetics model as below [33]:
(2)
ln
(
Q s
−
Q t
)
= ln
Q s
−
K 1
t
where QS refers to the saturated absorption capacity of PTPU and PTPU/CDs on soybean oil. Qt is the corresponding absorption capacity at the absorption time t. K1 refers to the absorption rate constant.

Toluene-in-water emulsion stabilized by Tween 80 is commonly used as simulated pollutant to test the oil-water separation performance of TPU sponge [14]. 0.02 g of emulsifier Tween 80 was added to 99 mL of deionized water, and stirred for three minutes. 10 mL of toluene was added to 40 mL of the above solution and stirred at high speed for 3 h to generate a milky oil-in-water emulsion. The prepared toluene-in-water emulsion with 20% v/v could maintain the stability at least for 5 days (Figure S1). The above emulsion was diluted at ratios of 1:32, 1:64, 1:128, 1:256, and 1:512 to obtain toluene-water emulsions with different concentrations. Using Tween 80-water solution as blank control, the absorption spectra of above toluene-in-water emulsions were tested using UV–Vis spectrophotometer to obtain the absorption intensities at 261 nm for different volume ratios of toluene-in-water emulsions [34]. A standard curve was plotted to determine the relationship between the toluene concentrations and absorption intensities of the emulsions. A piece of PTPU/CDs with an effective filtration area of 1.77 cm2 and a mass of approximately 0.3 g was affixed to the filtration device (Figure S2). Subsequently, 5 mL of toluene-in-water emulsion (20% v/v) was introduced into the microfilter cup. The filtration process could be completed within around 1 min by utilizing a pump vacuum filtration system. Subsequently, the absorption intensity of the filtrate at 261 nm was measured without any post-treatment to determine the toluene content in the filtrate (v/v) and to calculate the separation efficiency.

The separation efficiency of PTPU/CD porous materials for oil-in-water emulsion separation was calculated using the following equation:
(3)
R
( % )
=
(
1 −
C P
C F
)
×
100 %
where CF is the concentration of organic matter in the oil-water emulsion mixture before filtration and CP refers to the concentration of organic matter in the filtrate.

The microstructures of the PTPU and PTPU/CDs materials were characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). UV–vis absorbance tests were recorded on SHIMADZU UV-2600. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer. The mechanical property tests were conducted on a Moxin-0350 tension machine equipped with a 2 kN sensor. Water contact angles (WCA) were determined by using a Kruss DSA100 instrument. The morphology of CDs was observed using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested by using a PerkinElme FluoroMax-4 spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was preformed on Discovery TGA Q50 apparatus with a temperature rate of 10 °C/min in a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of PTPU and PTPU/CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Figure S3 shows the typical steps in the preparation of PTPU materials via the TIPS method, in which parameters including polymer concentration, phase separation time, phase separation temperature, and the ratio of good to poor solvent were investigated. Detailed formulas for these parameters can be found in Table S1. Samples with polymer concentrations of 4%, 6%, 8% and 10% (w/v) were prepared to investigate the effect of concentration on the pore structure. PTPU prepared with 4% and 6% polymer concentration exhibited a three-dimensional connected pore structure, but with fewer through pores, which was related to the faster separation speed due to the low viscosity of the system at low concentration (Figure S4a and S4b). PTPU with a concentration of 10% lacked large holes and exhibited a honeycomb-like structure consisting of mostly closed small pores with several microns in diameter (Figure S4d). This may account for that a high concentration system may result in the entanglement of TPU molecular chains which would prevent the generation of through pore structures. In contrast, PTPU with an 8% polymer concentration displayed a three-dimensional hollow structure, possessing excellent pore connectivity with a pore size ranging from 20 to 150 μm (Figure S4c).

Further investigation found that while fixing the polymer concentration at 8% and increasing the phase separation temperature from 0°C to 5°C, the PTPU material still has a good three-dimensional connected pore structure, but the pore diameter distribution was uneven (Figure S5b). When the phase separation temperature further rose to 10°C, the pores possessed smaller diameters and were mostly closed or unconnected (Figure S5c). This is owing to that the relatively high solubility of PTPU at high temperatures would lead to incomplete phase separation and that a portion of the polymer dissolved in the solvent would block the connectivity among the pores after the solvent was removed. From Figure S5d, while the phase separation time became shorter (5 min), the sample exhibited a closed pore morphology, with almost no connected pores due to the incomplete phase separation. The influence of volume ratio of 1,4-dioxane to water at 85:15, 90:10 and 95:5 on the PTPU microstructure was further investigated. In Figure S6, it can be found that when the volume ratio was 85:15, the pore size and the proportion of closed pores increased. Conversely, a 95:5 solvent ratio produced PTPU possessed a more regular microstructure, evenly distributed pore sizes, good connectivity between pores, and micro and nano pores on the surface of the framework. Thus, based on the aforementioned results, PTPU material with optimal pore morphology, pore size distribution, and a three-dimensional connectivity structure can be achieved with polymer concentration of 8% (w/v), phase separation temperature of 0°C, phase separation time of 30 min, and 1,4-dioxane to water ratio of 95:5.

Hydrophobic CDs, served as modifier, were utilized to improve the pore structure, mechanical property, surface roughness and hydrophobic properties of the PTPU material. The CDs were prepared through a solvent-free method and added to the precursor of PTPU in varying amounts according to the above optimized procedure. Fig. 1 a shows the photoluminescence characteristic of the CDs. When excited at 360 nm, the CDs dispersed in dichloromethane demonstrated a clear fluorescence emission peak at 552 nm. Optical image shows that the CDs solution exhibited a pale-yellow liquid under visible light, but emitted significant yellow fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation. It can be seen from the TEM image that CDs have an average diameter between 5 and 35 nm (Fig. 1b). The large size of the CDs may account for the aggregation in the solvent-free preparation process. From Fig. 1c, it can be seen that the PTPU material without the addition of CDs did not exhibit fluorescence, but only exhibited a faint blue fluorescence of the raw polymer. However, the PTPU/CDs composite material exhibited a clear bright yellow fluorescence with photoluminescence (PL) peak at 548 nm. The slight blue shift of the fluorescence may be caused by the transformation of CDs into a solid state. The FTIR results also suggest the successful thermal decomposition of ODA and the loading of CDs into PTPU (Figure S7). Additionally, the enhanced thermal stability observed in the PTPU/CDs further imply the strong interaction between CDs and PTPU chains (Figure S8).

From the SEM images of PTPU/CDs (Fig. 1d-f), we can find that the roughness of all the three samples has improved significantly. The introduction of hydrophobic CDs into the TPU could effectively reduce surface energy and expedite phase separation, thereby yielding more embryonic skeleton, resulting in the improved pore structures. PTPU/CDs with the addition of 3 wt% of CDs (mass to polymer) have small pores and poor connectivity. The samples with 1 and 2 wt% CDs show similar hollow structures with size from submicrons to tens of microns. In addition, the PTPU/CDs-2 composites exhibited the presence of pores larger than 50 μm, which were not observed in pure PTPU, thereby enhancing the oil loading capacity. The water contact angle of PTPU/CDs samples showed a significant increase compared with pure PTPU samples (Fig. 2 ). The PTPU/CDs samples with the addition of 2 wt% CDs had the highest water contact angle of 138.1°, indicating the most ideal pore structure and desirable hydrophobic properties. The increase in hydrophobicity of the PTPU/CDs can be attributed to the reduction in surface energy and the enhancement of surface roughness resulting from the incorporation of the hydrophobic CDs. The failure to achieve superhydrophobicity of the CDs modified PTPU materials may be attributed to the presence of hydrophilic -OH and -NH2 groups in the CDs, which are derived from citric acid and n-octadecylamine. The PTPU/CDs with 2 wt% CDs (denoted as PTPU/CDs-2) was used as the model sample in the oil-water separation performance test.

The mechanical properties have a significant impact on the durability of oil absorption materials. Fig. 3 shows that both pure PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2 exhibited good compressive elastic recovery after being compressed up to a maximum of 80% strain within ten cycles. The presence of CDs within the PTPU framework could potentially influence the mechanical properties through hydrogen bonding interactions between the hydroxyl and amino in CDs and imide in TPU. Compared with the pure PTPU, the compressive strength of PTPU/CDs-2 increased from 186 kPa to 233 kPa, indicating that CDs can effectively improve the mechanical properties of PTPU and implying their beneficial impact on the following recyclability and durability tests.

Saturated absorption capacity of various organic solvents and oils was measured with PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2. Fig. 4 a shows that the saturated absorption capacity of pure PTPU was 2.4–23 times of its own weight, while that of PTPU/CDs-2 was 4–39 times of its weight, indicating better absorption of PTPU/CDs-2 for the same solvents or oil. Particularly, the saturated absorption capacity of PTPU/CDs-2 for tetrachloromethane was 3 times that of PTPU, increasing from 8.3 g −1 to 25 g −1. PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2 exhibited excellent absorption performance for chloroform due to swelling effect. The relatively low saturated absorption capacities for oils with high viscosity may be attributed to the presence of small-sized pores, which hinder the infiltration of oil into certain spaces of the pore structures within PTPU/CDs. The oil saturated absorption capacity, including an overview of the saturated absorption capacity and the saturated absorption capacity for typical solvents, between PTPU/CDs and other materials previously reported is presented in Table S2. We observed that fabric-based oil absorption materials, such as FePA/FeOH@PAN [35] and PAM-modified polyester fabric [36], exhibit higher saturated absorption capacity for highly viscous oils, particularly vegetable oil. Conversely, our PTPU/CDs composites demonstrate superior saturated absorption capacity for hydrocarbon solvents. The PTPU/CDs composites exhibit higher saturated absorption capacity compared to other material-based sponges except melamine sponges, which may be attributed to their relatively low wettability. Furthermore, the CDs modified PTPU exhibits similar saturated absorption capacity to other carbon-based materials modified PTPU. However, the easily attainable nature of CDs along with their environmentally friendly properties and fluorescence tracing capabilities make them highly promising for applications in oil-water separation. By simple squeezing method, organic solvents removal can be achieved by PTPU/CDs-2 (Fig. 4b). The stability during recycled tests with peanut oil as an example of the PTPU/CDs materials on saturation absorption capacity is illustrated in Fig. 4c. The observed variation in saturation absorption capacity in each test can be attributed to the incomplete removal of absorbed oils through simple squeezing, primarily due to their relatively high viscosity. However, over 30 absorption/extrusion cycles, the saturation absorption capacity remained stable within the range of 5.7–6.8 g −1, demonstrating excellent circulation durability. The cycling separation efficiency of PTPU/CDs-2 composite material for oil pollutants floating on the water surface was studied. The pollutants can be removed nearly completely by repeated absorption and squeezing (Fig. 4d). The excellent compression elasticity of PTPU/CDs-2 endows the composite with the capability of facile oil/organic solvent removal through simple repeated absorption and squeezing process. The separation efficiency was determined by the accumulated change of the mass of PTPU/CDs-2 composite material after absorption to the initial mass of soybean oil on the water surface. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the PTPU/CDs-2 exhibited consistent and exceptional performance in removing oil pollutants from water surfaces throughout all 30 testing cycles with a separation efficiency consistently exceeding 99%, demonstrating remarkable stability and durability.

The environmental adaptability of PTPU/CDs-2 has also been investigated. The absorption effect of PTPU/CDs-2 on tetrachloromethane at the bottom of water can also be achieved (Fig. 4f). In addition, the absorption effect of PTPU/CDs-2 materials on peanut oil was observed under magnetic stirring. It can be found that even under severe stirring, PTPU/CDs-2 materials still exhibited a stable oil absorption effect, as shown in Fig. 4g. Droplets with different pH were subsequently dropped onto the PTPU/CDs-2 surface (Fig. 4h), and the PTPU/CDs-2 still exhibited a stable contact angle. Therein, the prepared PTPU/CDs-2 exhibited excellent environmental adaptability.

The PTPU material loaded with CDs achieves unique fluorescence properties simultaneously, which makes it possible to track its oil absorption process by fluorescence feature. As shown in Fig. 5 , the oil absorption process of PTPU/CDs-2 composite can be observed under natural light. When natural light disappears, PTPU/CDs-2 material can still be identified by the fluorescence by applying ultraviolet light. Therefore, it shows potential applications in specific environments such as dark conditions. It is worth mentioning that although our products do not achieve superhydrophobicity, they still demonstrate practicality for applications in oil/water separation based on their performance, particularly their special tracking ability under ultraviolet light as demonstrated in our study.

Figure S9 shows that the pseudo-first-order kinetic model explains well the absorption processes of soybean oil on PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2 materials. The slope of the fitting curve represents the absorption rate constant K1, and a larger K1 indicates a faster absorption. The absorption rate constant K1 of the PTPU/CDs-2 material is 0.039, which is about 1.5 times higher than that of the unmodified PTPU material (0.0262). The increase in absorption rate may due to the fact that the driving force for absorption comes from the capillary force of the pore structures [37] and the presence of CDs on the surface of PTPU can significantly increase surface roughness, improve the oil-wettability, amplify capillary action, thereby enhancing oil separation performance.

Toluene emulsified by Tween 80 surfactant in the water was used as simulated pollutant. The mixture was added to a microfilter cup in the filtration device (Figure S2). Toluene, acting as the dispersed phase, was effectively entrapped within the PTPU/CDs matrix during vacuum filtration owing to the presence of small-sized pores and the strong affinity towards the surface of PTPU/CDs. Water, as the continuous phase, permeated the membrane and was collected into a conical flask below. Fig. 6 a and b show a comparison of photos of the emulsion before and after filtration. It can be seen that the original solution was a stable milky liquid. Under an optical microscope, a large number of micrometer-sized droplets can be observed. However, the liquid obtained after filtration became clear and transparent, and almost all of the droplets disappeared, confirming that the organic solvents in the filtrate had been separated during the filtration process. The absorbance of the toluene-in-water emulsion with different concentrations was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The absorption intensity of the emulsion at 261 nm increased linearly with the toluene concentration rising, as presented in Fig. 6c and d. The calculated separation efficiency for toluene-in-water emulsion pollutants with PTPU/CDs-2 as filter membrane was 99.6% according to the fitted equation. Therefore, CDs modified PTPU material can effectively realize the oil/water separation of emulsifier-stabilized oily wastewater.

In conclusion, the preparation process for PTPU materials via thermally-induced phase separation was studied in detail and PTPU/CDs composite materials with excellent pore structure, improved wettability, mechanical property and oil-water separation performance were obtained through modifying the PTPU with hydrophobic CDs. Specifically, the PTPU/CDs-2 with 2wt% CDs modification exhibited significant improved pore structure and hydrophobicity (from WCA120° to WCA138.1°). In addition, the PTPU/CDs-2 show enhanced compressive strength (from 186 kPa to 233 kPa) compared with pure PTPU. Through saturation absorption testing, PTPU/CDs-2 exhibited increased saturation absorption capacity for various solvents compared to pure PTPU. Additionally, through cyclic performance testing, PTPU/CDs-2 demonstrated excellent durability and stability in both saturation absorption capacity and oil-water separation efficiency and was able to achieve oil separation in various environmental situations. Also, the addition of CDs endowed the PTPU/CDs-2 with fluorescence properties, enabling the tracking of its oil absorption process under UV irradiation. Using soybean oil as a model pollutant, through the study of the absorption kinetics of PTPU and PTPU/CDs-2, it was found that the PTPU/CDs-2 material exhibited an approximately 1.5-fold increase in absorption rate. In addition, the CDs modified PTPU could be used for separating oily wastewater stabilized by surfactant and the separation efficiency of toluene-in-water emulsion reached up to 99.6%. Overall, the CDs modified PTPU materials demonstrated impressive abilities in oil-water separation, paving a new way for the treatment of oily wastewater.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LQ22B060001)
Natural Science Fund Project of Colleges in Jiangsu Province (22KJB540002)

ce:section.ce:para: Yuting Ma: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lan Pan: Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Investigation. Kejin Jiang: Validation, Investigation. Qianhua Wang: Validation, Investigation. Zhijie Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yu Tian: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LQ22B060001, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (21062256-Y), Natural Science Fund Project of Colleges in Jiangsu Province (22KJB540002) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691676).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ceja.2023.100557.
